description: This figure presents thermodynamic calculations for Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel using Thermo-Calc, comprising four panels: (a) a pseudo-binary phase diagram (temperature vs. carbon content) showing phase stability regions for liquid (L), δ-ferrite, austenite (γ), and κ-carbide, with marked peritectic and eutectoid reactions; (b) phase fraction variations versus temperature (750–1200°C) revealing the disorder-order transition from δ-ferrite to B2 intermetallic at 1125°C and the eutectoid reaction at 851°C; (c) equilibrium solute distribution in the γ-matrix showing Mn (~17 wt%), Al (~8.5 wt%), Ni (~3 wt%), and C (~1.5 wt%) concentrations; and (d) solute partitioning into the B2-phase demonstrating enrichment of Al (up to ~14.5 wt%), Ni (up to ~12 wt%), and Mn while depicting negligible carbon solubility. The calculations explain the retention of 27% δ-ferrite after solidification due to incomplete peritectic reaction and its subsequent transformation into B2 stringer bands during thermomechanical processing, providing fundamental guidance for the annealing treatment at 900°C to optimize microstructural control and mechanical properties.

This figure illustrates the microstructural evolution of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel across critical processing stages. Optical micrographs (a–c) document the progressive transformation of as-cast dendritic δ-ferrite (approximately 27% volume fraction) within the austenite (γ) matrix into continuous lamellar B2 stringer bands following hot rolling (HR), with further thickness reduction and elongation observed after subsequent cold rolling (CR). The field-emission SEM image (d) reveals fine intragranular B2 (IG-B2) precipitates nucleated preferentially at shear bands formed within the deformed γ matrix during HR. Together, these images establish that controlled thermomechanical processing converts coarse solidification δ-ferrite into uniformly distributed B2 intermetallic phases, with IG-B2 precipitation occurring at strain-localized sites, thereby tailoring the microstructure for enhanced strength-ductility synergy.

This figure presents field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images revealing the microstructural evolution of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel annealed at 900°C for varying durations (5, 15, and 30 min, labeled A5, A15, A30). The top row (a–c) at lower magnification shows lamellar B2 stringer bands transformed from δ-ferrite and unrecrystallized austenite (URE-γ) maintaining a pancake structure elongated along the rolling direction. The middle row (d–f) and bottom row (g–i) at progressively higher magnifications illustrate the precipitation behavior of B2 intermetallic phase: intragranular B2 (IG-B2, blue arrows) nucleates heterogeneously within URE-γ at shear bands, appearing finer in A5 and coarsening into needle-like shapes through coalescence upon extended annealing in A30; whereas intergranular B2 (GB-B2, green arrows) precipitates as coarse, irregular particles along prior austenite grain boundaries. The images demonstrate that with increasing annealing time, IG-B2 grows larger while GB-B2 remains coarse, providing direct evidence for the precipitation strengthening mechanisms discussed in the study.

Figure 4 presents bright-field TEM micrographs and corresponding TEM-EDX elemental mappings of sample A30 (regular processing route, annealed at 900°C for 30 min) to characterize B2 intermetallic precipitation and solute partitioning. Panel (a) reveals irregular GB-B2 nucleating along prior austenite grain boundaries, needle-shaped IG-B2 within unrecrystallized austenite grains, and sporadic annealing twins; the inset specifically demonstrates heterogeneous nucleation of B2 at twin plate boundaries. Panels (b)-(f) illustrate elemental distributions: Fe (b), Al (e), and Ni (f) exhibit distinct enrichment within B2 precipitates, whereas Mn (c) and C (d) show marked depletion. The negligible carbon solubility in B2 confirms its identity as an intermetallic phase with chemical formula Fe(Ni,Mn)Al rather than a carbide. Notably, Ni enrichment in B2 occurs despite its typical role as a γ-stabilizer, indicating substitution of Fe-Al bonds with stronger Ni-Al bonds that stabilize the B2 structure. These observations provide direct evidence for solute partitioning during annealing and establish the crystallographic nature of B2 precipitates.

Figure 5 presents engineering stress–strain curves and fracture micrographs of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni low-density steel processed by hot rolling (HR), cold rolling (CR), and annealing at 900°C for 5–30 min (A5–A30). Panel (a) shows CR achieves ultrahigh strength (~1500 MPa) but negligible ductility, while HR exhibits ~6.8% elongation. Annealing increases ductility but yields inconsistent strength–toughness balance, with toughness rising from 0.11 kJ/mol (CR) to 0.89 kJ/mol (A30) per the inset table. Panels (b–e) comprise SEM and optical micrographs revealing failure mechanisms at the tensile gauge: (b) SEM of A5 shows B2 stringer de-cohesion; (c) optical image of A10 displays micro-cracks perpendicular to the loading axis in thick B2 stringers; (d) SEM of A15 exhibits void nucleation within B2 bands; and (e) SEM of A30 demonstrates micro-crack merger and stringer band annihilation. Panel (f) is an EBSD orientation map of A30 revealing grain boundaries within recrystallized bamboo-like B2 stringer bands. The figure establishes that premature failure in conventionally processed samples arises from B2 stringer decohesion and microvoid coalescence at grain boundaries, explaining the limited toughness.

This figure presents the mechanical properties and microstructural deformation features of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel processed via modified high-speed hot rolling (10 rpm) followed by cold rolling and annealing at 900°C for varying durations. Panel (a) displays engineering stress–strain curves comparing modified samples A5, A15, and A30 (annealed for 5, 15, and 30 min, respectively) against conventional HR and CR conditions. The modified A5 exhibits the highest yield strength (~1 GPa), while A30 demonstrates the optimal toughness of 1.72 kJ mol⁻¹ (238.38 J cm⁻³) with superior ductility (~30% elongation), as detailed in the inset table. Panel (b) presents Holloman plots (lnσ versus lnε) revealing two-stage strain hardening behavior with strain hardening exponents (n) transitioning from initially low values (0.054–0.069) to higher steady-state values (0.179–0.27). Panels (c)–(e) are optical micrographs of deformed tensile gauge sections showing B2 stringer bands (Fe-Al intermetallic phase) and micro-crack formation in A5, A15, and A30, respectively, illustrating reduced crack susceptibility due to refined B2 stringer band fractions (from 16% to 11%) and enhanced austenite matrix deformation capacity. The figure collectively demonstrates that process control through high-speed rolling refines the microstructure, enabling strain partitioning between softer austenite and harder B2 phases to achieve ultrahigh strength-ductility synergy comparable to TRIP-aided steels.

This figure presents multimodal electron microscopy analysis of microstructural deformation in Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel, comparing cold-rolled (CR) conditions and sample A15 (conventionally processed and annealed at 900°C for 15 min) after interrupted tensile testing at plastic strain e = 0.05. Panels (a) and (b) show EBSD phase and Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps of the CR condition, revealing co-deformation of B2 stringer bands and austenite (γ) with strain localization predominantly in the softer γ phase. Panel (c) provides a TEM bright-field image of A15, confirming plastic deformation in both γ and B2 with higher dislocation density in γ. Panels (d) and (e) display high-resolution EBSD phase and KAM maps of A15, visualizing geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) localization at γ/B2 interfaces and γ grain boundaries. The images demonstrate strain partitioning between the hard B2 intermetallic and soft γ matrix, where dislocations localize at phase interfaces, reducing GND density in the bulk γ (64% volume fraction) and contributing to yield point elongation and reduced strain hardening exponents (0.19–0.27).
